headline: The Coding Agents Fight Over the Meter
deck: The Claude-versus-Codex race has moved from which model writes better code to who controls the meter. Anthropic keeps hard hourly and weekly caps; OpenAI pools tokenised credits. With a temporary Claude limit boost expiring, developers are routing around it.

art.caption: The coding-agent war is being fought on the meter now, not the benchmark: hourly caps, weekly resets, and pooled credits.

body: The fight between the two leading coding agents has quietly changed subject. For a year the question was whether Anthropic's Claude or OpenAI's Codex wrote better code; this month the argument developers are actually having is about limits, resets and quotas. Both companies now let heavy users pay through their caps, but they do it with opposite psychology, and that difference has become the real product war [E1].

Anthropic's system is quota-first and explicit. Claude usage runs on a shared budget across its apps, with five-hour session limits, weekly limits, fixed reset times and paid usage credits once a user hits the wall. That legibility is also its liability: developers know exactly when they will be cut off, and a temporary boost to Claude Code's limits is set to expire around 13 July, with the strong Fable model sliding from included access to metered credits at the same time [E1]. The result online is cancellation threats and a running complaint that Claude is "unusable" for long agentic sessions that die mid-task.

OpenAI is pulling the other way, toward a pooled, tokenised budget. Its Codex credits stretch across the terminal, ChatGPT and even spreadsheet workflows, billed per token with automatic top-up, so limits feel less like a wall and more like a spend dial. The commercial endpoint is the same, power users pay more, but the experience reads as flexible rather than rationed. OpenAI says more than five million people use Codex every week, and it folded the newly released GPT-5.6 directly into that runtime [E1].

Developer affection and developer reach are not the same thing, and right now they are split. Claude Code leads the public enthusiasm metrics, with far more GitHub stars than Codex, and remains the cultural object among terminal-native programmers. Codex leads distribution through ChatGPT's mainstream base. The tell of the moment is the workaround the two produced together: a hybrid setup, nicknamed "claudex," in which developers keep the Claude interface they like but point it at OpenAI's model to escape the caps [E1].

The war is also acquiring a border. China's industry ministry issued a security alert over an alleged backdoor risk in Claude Code, a move Anthropic did not confirm, but one that pushes the coding-agent contest along the same geopolitical software-trust lines that now split every other layer of the stack [E2]. Domestic Chinese tools stand to gain from any distrust of American agents, and Chinese labs are already optimising their models to plug into the same harnesses developers use.

The near-term signal is unglamorous and precise. Watch whether Anthropic softens its weekly-cap language after the 13 July expiry, and whether OpenAI extends the pooled-credit model into its business defaults rather than the current eligibility patchwork [E1]. Benchmarks will keep making headlines, but the customers who spend the most now choose their agent on the meter, and the meter is where this war will be won [E1].

dissent.argument: A limits war is a sign of demand, not distress; both companies are rationing scarce compute, and today's rate-limit anger converts into next quarter's higher-tier revenue rather than an exodus.

evidence_box.fragment: OpenAI's broad GPT-5.6 rollout folded into Codex; 5M+ weekly Codex users; Anthropic's five-hour and weekly Claude limits, usage credits, and Claude Code limit-boost expiry
China issues a "backdoor" security alert over Anthropic's Claude Code
